did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper evaluated the external validity and reliability of two AI algorithms (Han et al., 2018 12-disease algorithm and Han et al., 2020b 174-disease algorithm, plus a 178-disease region-based algorithm) for classifying skin lesions. The study used these deep learning models to obtain diagnostic results (top 1, top 3, and top any accuracy) for melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma on a clinical image dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is titled 'LETTERS TO THE EDITOR' but contains original experimental results. The authors state they 'evaluated the external validity and reliability' of specific AI algorithms (Han et al., 2020b and Han et al., 2020a) using a public dataset of 100 clinical images. They report specific quantitative results including top 1, top 3, and top any accuracies, sensitivity, and statistical comparisons (P-values) across different upload conditions. Although published as a letter, it presents new primary data and analysis rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Multiclass Artificial Intelligence in Dermatology: Progress but Still Room for Improvement
brief_summary: This study evaluated the external validity and reliability of two artificial intelligence algorithms, a 174-disease classifier (Han et al., 2020b) and a 178-disease region-based classifier (Han et al., 2020a), using a public dataset of 100 clinical images containing melanomas, basal cell carcinomas, and squamous cell carcinomas. The researchers tested the algorithms under four different upload conditions to assess performance sensitivity to image magnification and region of interest selection. The 174-disease algorithm achieved an overall top 1 accuracy of 32% and a melanoma top 1 accuracy of 29.7%, showing modest improvements over a previous 12-disease version but limited transportability. The 178-disease region-based algorithm demonstrated a top any accuracy of 52%, with performance varying significantly by anatomic site and lesion proximity. The results indicate that while AI progress has been made, classification performance remains sensitive to user input variations and anatomic location, suggesting current algorithms are better suited as clinical decision support tools rather than autonomous diagnostic systems.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (skin diseases) and malignancy status
all_task_types.details: The study evaluated two AI algorithms for multiclass classification of skin lesions. The Han et al. (2020b) algorithm classified images into 174 disease categories, reporting top 1 and top 3 accuracies for specific classes (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma). The Han et al. (2020a) algorithm performed region-based classification of 178 diseases using a blob detector and disease classifier, reporting top any accuracy. Both tasks involve predicting discrete diagnostic labels rather than continuous numeric outcomes.

datasets_used.name: Public clinical image dataset of skin cancer (Navarrete-Dechent et al., 2018)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of 100 sequentially biopsied clinical images of skin cancer from Caucasian patients in the United States. The dataset was originally sourced from the ISIC archive (specifically the 2018 JID editorial images). It contains cutaneous melanomas, basal cell carcinomas, and squamous cell carcinomas. All lesions were located on the head and neck, trunk, upper extremities, or lower extremities.

use.testing: Used as the external validation dataset to evaluate the external validity and reliability of the Han et al. (2020b) 174-disease algorithm and the Han et al. (2020a) 178-disease region-based algorithm.
use.other: The dataset was uploaded to the respective web apps (https://modelderm.com/ and http://rcnn-modelderm.com/) on specific dates in April and June 2020 for testing under four different upload conditions.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Comparisons of proportions in contingency tables (e.g., anatomic site association with top 1 accuracy, performance differences between close-up and distant lesions).
Comparisons of proportions in contingency tables (e.g., anatomic site association with top 1 accuracy, performance differences between close-up and distant lesions).
Significance thresholds for statistical comparisons of accuracy rates and proportions across different upload conditions and anatomic sites.

hypothesis_testing.test: Chi-square test and Fisher’s exact test
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no association between the categorical variables being compared (e.g., anatomic site and top 1 accuracy, or upload condition and accuracy rates).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant association between the categorical variables being compared.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: P-values were used to determine statistical significance for comparisons of proportions in contingency tables. Significant differences were found for melanoma top 1 accuracy across diagnoses (P = 0.01), overall top 1 accuracy under upload conditions (P = 0.07, though not significant at 0.05), melanoma top 1 accuracy under upload conditions (P = 0.008), top 1 accuracy by anatomic site for intended use off-center uploads (P = 0.006), and top 1 accuracy by anatomic site combining all uploads (P < 0.001). No significant association was found for the 178-disease algorithm's performance by anatomic site (P = 0.547) or between close-up and distant lesions (P = 0.08).

Public clinical image dataset of skin ca.description: A public dataset of 100 sequentially biopsied clinical images of skin cancer from Caucasian patients in the United States (37 cutaneous melanomas, 40 basal cell carcinomas, 23 squamous cell carcinomas).

Intended Use.malignancy_probability_mean_SD: 41.1% (SD not reported for this specific condition in table, but mean is 41.1)

Han et al. (2018) 12-Disease Algorithm.notes: The 174-disease algorithm (Han et al., 2020b) outperformed this 12-disease version. Overall top 1 accuracy improved from 39% to 32% (P=0.38) and top 3 from 63% to 60% (P=0.77) when comparing the 12-disease intended use to the 174-disease intended use. Melanoma top 1 accuracy increased from 13.5% to 29.7% (P=0.16) and top 3 from 35.1% to 56.7% (P=0.10).

Intended Use.malignancy_probability_mean_SD: 41.1% (SD not explicitly separated in table row but listed as 41.1)

statistical_comparisons.top_1_accuracy_by_anatomic_site_combined: P < 0.001 (Lower extremities 18% vs. all other sites 36%)

Han et al. (2020b) 174-Disease Algorithm.notes: Performance was best with intended use uploads (similar to off-center) and drag-and-drop. Worst performance was with minus 1 magnification. No associations between rulers, clothing, and hair were identified with accuracy.

Han et al. (2020a) 178-Disease Region-Ba.notes: The algorithm had low sensitivity for skin cancer. Performance was highest on the head and neck and lowest on the lower extremities. Close-up lesions performed better than distant lesions.

architecture_used.main_model: Two algorithms were evaluated: 1) Han et al. (2020b) 174-disease classifier; 2) Han et al. (2020a) 178-disease region-based algorithm (described as a region-based convolutional neural network with a blob detector, fine image selector, and disease classifier).
architecture_used.pretraining: Not reported in the provided text.
architecture_used.input_size: For the 174-disease algorithm, users could select a 250-by-250 pixel square region of interest (ROI). For the 178-disease algorithm, no specific input size was reported, but it processed images via a blob detector.

174_disease_algorithm.input_processing: Users select a 250x250 pixel ROI by scaling down the image (0-1x magnification) and panning within the view window.

178_disease_algorithm.optimization_target: Classification of images of the face
178_disease_algorithm.output: Top any accuracy across multiple blobs per lesion

training_details.training_data: Not reported in the provided text (referenced as Han et al., 2020a and 2020b).

174_disease_algorithm.malignancy_score: Sum of probability outputs for melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma

178_disease_algorithm.output_type: Probabilities for 178 disease classes per detected blob

preprocessing_techniques: Region of Interest (ROI) selection: Users selected a 250-by-250 pixel square ROI by scaling down the image (between 0 and 1 times magnification) and panning within the image view window.
Intended use upload: ROI centered the lesion and was approximately 80% covered by the lesion.
Intended use off-center upload: ROI was adjusted to off-center the lesion.
Drag and drop upload: Images submitted at the magnification selected by the web app.
Minus 1 magnification upload: Lesion centered and covered as much of the image as possible (closest to -1 magnification).
Blob detection: The 178-disease region-based algorithm used a blob detector to identify multiple partially overlapping blobs for each lesion.
Fine image selector: The 178-disease algorithm used a fine image selector to process detected blobs.
No explicit preprocessing: For the 178-disease algorithm, no magnification or ROI modification was performed by the user; the algorithm processed images via its internal blob detector and classifier.
conclusions: The 174-disease AI algorithm showed modest improvements over the previous 12-disease version (overall top 1 accuracy 32% vs 39%, P=0.38; melanoma top 1 accuracy 29.7% vs 13.5%, P=0.16) but demonstrated limited transportability to an external dataset.

Classification performance of the 174-disease algorithm was sensitive to perturbations in image magnification, with the worst performance observed at -1 magnification (top 1 accuracy 24%), implying users must follow intended use instructions.

Anatomic site was significantly associated with top 1 accuracy for the 174-disease algorithm, with inferior performance on the lower extremities (18%) compared to all other sites combined (36%, P < 0.001).

The 178-disease region-based algorithm had low sensitivity for skin cancer and a top any accuracy of 52%, with performance varying by anatomic site (highest on head and neck at 58%, lowest on lower extremities at 39%) and lesion distance (better for close-up lesions at 58% vs distant at 38%).

Both algorithms are currently better suited for use as clinical decision support tools rather than autonomous diagnostic systems due to limited transportability and sensitivity to user input variations.
The study is limited by its retrospective design and the use of a small dataset composed of few diagnostic classes.

Strategies to improve AI performance include establishing standards for acquiring dermatology images to improve quality and generalizability, and training algorithms with greater diversity of image types and settings.
important_remarks: The study is limited by its retrospective design and the use of a small dataset (n=100) composed of few diagnostic classes.
The 174-disease algorithm demonstrated limited transportability to an external dataset, with modest improvements over the previous 12-disease version (overall top 1 accuracy 32% vs 39%, P=0.38).

Classification performance was highly sensitive to user input variations, particularly image magnification; the worst performance was observed at -1 magnification (top 1 accuracy 24%), indicating users must strictly follow intended use instructions.
Anatomic site significantly impacted performance for the 174-disease algorithm, with inferior top 1 accuracy on the lower extremities (18%) compared to all other sites combined (36%, P < 0.001).

The 178-disease region-based algorithm exhibited low sensitivity for skin cancer and a top any accuracy of only 52%, with performance varying by anatomic site (highest on head and neck at 58%, lowest on lower extremities at 39%).
The algorithms are currently better suited for clinical decision support rather than autonomous diagnostic systems due to limited transportability and sensitivity to perturbations.

No associations were found between rulers, clothing, and hair with accuracy for the 174-disease algorithm, but the 178-disease algorithm was optimized for facial images, potentially limiting generalizability to other body sites.
The dataset consisted exclusively of Caucasian patients from the United States, which may limit generalizability to other ethnicities or geographic regions.
